skill.name: pdf-analyze
skill.description: PDF processing toolkit for extracting text and tables, creating and modifying PDFs, converting pages to images, and filling PDF forms with local scripts and common PDF libraries.
skill.summary: Analyze, transform, and fill PDF documents using local Python and command-line workflows.
skill.author: 21pounder

security_audit.summary: Most static findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, example commands, geometry test data, and documentation URLs. A real issue remains: FORMS.md contains imperative prompt-control language directed at the model, and the bundled scripts read and write user-supplied file paths. No evidence found of credential theft, outbound exfiltration, obfuscation, or malware, so this is high risk but not a confirmed malicious block.

high_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
high_findings.description: FORMS.md contains untrusted instructions such as CRITICAL, MUST, and do not proceed directives that attempt to control model behavior. The language is workflow related, but it is still unsafe for marketplace publication without neutral wording.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious imperative language is directly present in untrusted skill content and is addressed to the assistant. It does not ask to bypass security review or exfiltrate data, so confidence is high but not absolute for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied File Path Reads and Writes
medium_findings.description: The bundled scripts read input PDFs and JSON files and write output PDFs, JSON, or images to paths supplied on the command line. This is expected for PDF processing, but it can overwrite files or process sensitive documents if run without path review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and use command-line paths. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but the filesystem impact is real.

medium_findings.title: User-Directed External PDF Commands
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs the assistant to run local Python scripts and common PDF command-line tools such as qpdf, pdftotext, and pdfimages. These are legitimate PDF workflows, but they execute local tools against user documents and require user trust in the inputs and outputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown and align with the skill purpose. They are not shell injection in code, but they are still operational instructions to execute local tools.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The static network alerts point to comments that cite PDF and library documentation. No code performs HTTP requests or sends PDF contents to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL strings occur only inside comments. I found no request, fetch, socket, or upload code using them.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and C2 Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and C2 keyword alerts are from PDF password examples, ordinary document-processing text, and bounding-box test data. No evidence found of cryptographic misuse, command-and-control behavior, or obfuscated malware logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is visible as examples or test fixtures, not security-sensitive cryptographic code. One example mentions PDF encryption, but it is user-facing PDF protection documentation rather than malware behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Are Not Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The many Ruby shell backtick alerts occur in Markdown examples and inline code formatting. The executable Python scripts do not use backticks, subprocess, os.system, or shell=True.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks delimit Markdown code blocks or inline examples. Review of the scripts found no actual shell execution primitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
dangerous_patterns.description: FORMS.md contains untrusted instructions such as CRITICAL, MUST, and do not proceed directives that attempt to control model behavior. The language is workflow related, but it is still unsafe for marketplace publication without neutral wording.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious imperative language is directly present in untrusted skill content and is addressed to the assistant. It does not ask to bypass security review or exfiltrate data, so confidence is high but not absolute for malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.description: The bundled scripts read input PDFs and JSON files and write output PDFs, JSON, or images to paths supplied on the command line. This is expected for PDF processing, but it can overwrite files or process sensitive documents if run without path review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and use command-line paths. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but the filesystem impact is real.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Directed External PDF Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation instructs the assistant to run local Python scripts and common PDF command-line tools such as qpdf, pdftotext, and pdfimages. These are legitimate PDF workflows, but they execute local tools against user documents and require user trust in the inputs and outputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown and align with the skill purpose. They are not shell injection in code, but they are still operational instructions to execute local tools.

content.value_statement: PDF workflows often require several libraries and careful form handling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for local PDF extraction, conversion, editing, and form filling.
actual_capabilities: Extract text, metadata, and tables from PDFs with pypdf and pdfplumber examples.
Merge, split, rotate, watermark, encrypt, decrypt, and repair PDFs using Python or command-line tools.
Render PDF pages to images for OCR, previews, and visual form analysis.
Detect fillable form fields and generate field metadata for review.
Fill PDF forms with validated field values or add text annotations to non-fillable forms.
Create new PDFs with ReportLab and JavaScript pdf-lib examples.
limitations: Requires local Python libraries and optional tools such as qpdf, poppler-utils, Tesseract, and pdfimages.
Scanned PDFs need OCR tooling and may produce imperfect text extraction results.
Form filling can require manual visual inspection to confirm field placement.
Bundled scripts accept user-supplied file paths and do not implement sandboxing.

use_cases.title: Prepare PDF Form Packets
use_cases.description: Inspect fillable fields, map values, and create completed forms for review.

use_cases.title: Extract Document Data
use_cases.description: Pull text, tables, images, and metadata from PDF reports for downstream analysis.

use_cases.title: Automate PDF Assembly
use_cases.description: Merge, split, rotate, watermark, and generate PDFs during repeatable document workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Basic PDF Text
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this PDF and extract the main text, page count, title, author, and any obvious tables. Note whether OCR may be needed.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Pages for Visual Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this PDF into page images, inspect the visual layout, and summarize any form fields or table regions that need manual review.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a PDF Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Determine whether this PDF has fillable fields. If it does, extract field information, map these provided values to fields, and create a completed PDF for review.

prompt_templates.title: Build a PDF Processing Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a local PDF workflow that extracts tables, splits selected pages, adds a watermark, and creates validation artifacts for every output.

output_examples.input: A three-page invoice PDF with a table and embedded metadata.
output_examples.output: A concise report listing page count, document metadata, extracted invoice table rows, and any pages that need OCR.

output_examples.input: A non-fillable application form plus user-provided answers.
output_examples.output: A filled PDF draft with text annotations and validation images that show where each answer was placed.

output_examples.input: Several PDFs that need to become one reviewed packet.
output_examples.output: A merged document, a list of source page ranges, and notes about rotations, watermarks, or repair steps applied.

best_practices: Review file paths before running scripts, especially output paths that can overwrite existing files.
Use validation images and manual inspection before trusting non-fillable form placement.
Keep original PDFs unchanged and write processed outputs to a separate working directory.
anti_patterns: Do not run the scripts on untrusted PDFs without local isolation and user approval.
Do not assume extracted text is complete when the PDF is scanned or image based.
Do not publish generated filled forms without reviewing field values and placement.

faq.question: Can this skill fill both fillable and non-fillable PDF forms?
faq.answer: Yes. It handles fillable fields directly and can add text annotations for non-fillable forms after visual field mapping.

faq.question: Does it send PDFs to an external service?
faq.answer: No evidence found of outbound uploads. The included scripts use local files and local Python libraries.

faq.question: What tools may need to be installed?
faq.answer: Common dependencies include pypdf, pdfplumber, reportlab, pypdfium2, pdf2image, poppler-utils, qpdf, and Tesseract for OCR.

faq.question: Can it extract tables from PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance includes pdfplumber examples for extracting tables and exporting structured data.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish as submitted?
faq.answer: No. The audit found untrusted prompt-control language that should be rewritten before marketplace publication.

faq.question: Can it process encrypted PDFs?
faq.answer: It includes examples for password-protected PDFs, but users must provide authorized passwords and verify the output.
